sub_problems.description: Define the target audience and their existing knowledge level for the AI Automation study roadmap
constraints: Should be applicable to real-world automation scenarios

sub_problems.description: Identify core AI automation technologies and their learning dependencies
constraints: Must balance foundational theory with practical tools

sub_problems.description: Structure progressive learning phases with measurable outcomes
constraints: Should include both conceptual and hands-on components

sub_problems.description: Design practical implementation projects that reinforce learning
constraints: Projects must be completable with reasonable effort

sub_problems.description: Create maintenance and advancement pathways for ongoing learning

assumptions.text: The requester wants a structured learning path rather than just a list of topics
assumptions.label: VERIFIED
assumptions.rationale: The term 'roadmap' implies progression and structure, not just enumeration

assumptions.text: The roadmap should cover both theoretical foundations and practical implementation
assumptions.label: HYPOTHESIS
assumptions.rationale: Automation requires both understanding AI concepts and applying them to real systems

assumptions.text: The requester has some technical background but may not be an AI expert
assumptions.label: UNKNOWN
assumptions.rationale: No information provided about the requester's existing knowledge

assumptions.text: AI Automation refers to using AI to automate business/technical processes, not just robotic process automation
assumptions.label: VERIFIED
assumptions.rationale: Contemporary usage of 'AI Automation' typically includes machine learning, NLP, computer vision applications

assumptions.text: The roadmap should be actionable within typical learning timeframes (months, not years)
assumptions.label: HYPOTHESIS
assumptions.rationale: Most learners seek practical skills acquisition within reasonable time investment

failure_modes: Overwhelming beginners with too many technologies simultaneously
Creating theoretical knowledge without transferable practical skills
Building roadmap around tools that become obsolete before implementation
Failing to address the integration challenges between AI components and existing systems
Assuming uniform learning pace across all audience members

candidates.content: This problem is fundamentally unsolvable as presented because 'AI Automation' is a moving target with no stable definition. The sub-problems assume a coherent domain exists when in reality it's a marketing term conflating at least four distinct fields: traditional RPA, machine learning workflows, cognitive automation, and AI-assisted development. SP2's 'technology taxonomy' will inevitably be obsolete before completion due to the 3-6 month innovation cycle in AI tools. The dependency graph will be impossibly complex—learning TensorFlow doesn't help with AutoGPT, which doesn't help with UiPath AI Center. The constraint 'avoid vendor lock-in' is laughable when every practical implementation requires proprietary platforms (OpenAI API, Microsoft Copilot Studio, etc.). SP3's 'measurable outcomes' are meaningless when success criteria change monthly—today's cutting-edge LangChain implementation is tomorrow's deprecated code. SP4's 'real-world use cases' will either be trivial (automating email sorting) or enterprise-scale (supply chain optimization) with no middle ground for learners. Most critically, the 12-18 month relevance constraint (SP5) is impossible; a roadmap created today will be 40% irrelevant in 6 months due to model updates, API changes, and new paradigm shifts (note the recent move from fine-tuning to prompt engineering to agent systems).
key_insights: The prerequisite analysis (SP1) fails because 'AI Automation' learners don't exist as a coherent group—they're either software engineers needing AI literacy or business analysts needing automation skills, with almost zero overlap in required foundations.

The phased curriculum (SP3) creates a false linear progression when real learning happens through parallel experimentation with unstable tools that frequently break, change interfaces, or disappear entirely.

The project specifications (SP4) will inevitably teach outdated patterns because by the time a project is designed, tested, and documented, the underlying AI services have already evolved beyond the project's assumptions.

candidates.content: The sub-problems present a linear, waterfall-style decomposition that fails to account for the emergent, adaptive nature of learning complex technological systems. While the structure appears logical (audience → technologies → curriculum → projects → maintenance), it creates several systemic vulnerabilities. First, the dependency chain assumes perfect information flow between stages—what happens when SP2's technology taxonomy reveals that SP1's audience prerequisites are insufficient? Second, the approach treats 'AI Automation' as a static domain when it's actually a moving target with rapidly shifting toolchains and paradigms. The feedback loops are inadequate: SP5's 'community feedback mechanisms' come far too late in the process to influence foundational decisions. More critically, the roadmap design doesn't account for the learner's own adaptation—their interests, capabilities, and goals will evolve as they progress, potentially making early-phase decisions obsolete. The constraints reveal tension points: 'balancing foundational theory with practical tools' (SP2) conflicts with 'projects must be completable with reasonable effort' (SP4), creating a classic theory-practice gap. The assumption that this can be 'actionable within months' while 'remaining relevant for 12-18 months' ignores the exponential change curve in AI technologies—what's foundational today may be deprecated in six months.
key_insights: The linear dependency structure creates a brittle system where early miscalculations propagate uncorrected through later phases—a more networked, iterative approach with continuous feedback between all sub-problems would be more resilient.

The roadmap treats 'learning' as content delivery rather than capability development—it's missing the metacognitive layer where learners develop judgment about which automation approaches work in which contexts, which is the true differentiator in real-world implementation.

The constraint 'should avoid vendor lock-in bias' in SP2 directly conflicts with the reality that most practical AI automation today happens through vendor platforms (OpenAI, Anthropic, AWS, etc.)—the tension between theoretical purity and practical implementation creates a hidden curriculum decision point that isn't explicitly addressed.

error: Processing error in critique & pruning phase

tests.failure_mode: No specific failure mode in optimal conditions, but minor inefficiencies may arise from over-reliance on theoretical knowledge without practical application, leading to gaps in real-world implementation skills.
tests.recovery_path: Implement iterative feedback loops with industry practitioners, incorporate hands-on projects early in the roadmap, and establish mentorship programs to bridge theory-practice gaps.

tests.failure_mode: Roadmap collapses due to lack of structured content (empty solution list), insufficient resources (limited access to tools/data), and partial data leading to knowledge gaps. Without specific learning paths or materials, learners cannot progress beyond basic concepts.
tests.recovery_path: Immediately populate roadmap with concrete, prioritized learning modules (e.g., Python fundamentals, ML libraries, automation frameworks). Leverage free/open-source resources (MOOCs, documentation). Implement peer study groups and project-based learning to compensate for resource constraints.

tests.failure_mode: Malicious actor systematically sabotages the roadmap by spreading misinformation (e.g., outdated tutorials, broken tools), creating fake learning communities, and poisoning training data/resources. This erodes trust and renders the roadmap unusable.
tests.recovery_path: Establish verified, curated resource repositories with version control and integrity checks. Implement community moderation and credential verification for contributors. Migrate to decentralized or tamper-evident platforms for content distribution, and foster critical thinking skills to help learners identify sabotage.
